reasoning: Following the pattern of 'na uluqu' (my head), body parts take the n-class possessive suffix -qu for first person singular
Kava is a drink/beverage, so it uses the me-class possessive. 'mena' = her (for drinks)
Witchcraft is alienable possession, using the n-class possessive 'noqu' for my
Rope (you own) is alienable possession, using n-class 'nomu' for your (sing.)
Fish for dinner is edible, using ke-class 'kemudrau' for your (dual)
Water is drinkable, using me-class 'memuni' for your (pl.)
Child uses alienable n-class possession 'nona' for her
Kava drunk in his honor uses me-class for drinks, 'mena' for his (third person singular)
From the example 'na keda wau' (our incl. weapon), canoe uses ke-class 'keda' for our (incl.)
Apple they're selling is alienable possession, using n-class 'nodra' for their
Witchcraft you're making uses dual ke-class 'kemudrau' for your (dual)
Rope restraining uses ke-class 'kemuni' for your (pl.)
Mango for drinking (juice) uses ke-class 'kedra' for their

answer: The phrase uses 'mena' which is the me-class (drink) possessive for third person singular. This indicates the kava belongs to him in the sense that it is being consumed for his benefit or in his honor, rather than simple ownership.
reasoning: Explaining the possessive class system and how me-class relates to consumption of drinks

answer: na noqu niu (I own it), na kequ niu (I will eat it), na mequ niu (I will drink from it/the liquid). These represent different types of possession: alienable ownership (n-class), food consumption (ke-class), and drink consumption (me-class).
reasoning: Coconut can be possessed in different ways depending on the relationship - ownership, eating the flesh, or drinking the water
